definition: The task of writing a natural-language description of an image, joining a visual encoder that reads the picture to a language decoder that writes the sentence. It is the mirror image of text-to-image generation and a long-standing probe of multimodal understanding, since captioning well proves the visual features carry enough structured meaning to be verbalized, and it has now largely folded into the vision-language model.

body.text: Image captioning is the task of looking at a picture and writing a sentence that describes it. Its input is an image and its output is fluent natural language, "a brown dog catching a frisbee on a grassy lawn", which makes it the mirror image of text-to-image generation: one turns pixels into words, the other turns words into pixels. It is a foundational test of joint visual and linguistic understanding, because producing a good caption requires recognizing the objects in the scene, inferring their attributes and relationships, and rendering all of that as grammatical, relevant prose rather than a bag of detected labels.

The task matters both as a capability and as a probe. As a capability it powers alternative text for accessibility, letting screen readers describe images to blind users, and it drives image search, content moderation, and photo organization. As a probe it stresses the seam where vision meets language, so captioning quality has long served as a barometer of multimodal learning progress: a model that captions well has, in effect, demonstrated that its visual features carry enough structured meaning to be verbalized. Generating language is also strictly harder than classifying, because the space of valid descriptions is open-ended and the output must be coherent over many words, not chosen from a fixed menu.

Architecturally, captioning follows an encoder-decoder, sequence-to-sequence shape adapted to a visual input. A visual encoder, historically a convolutional network and now usually a vision transformer, reads the image into a set of feature vectors; a language decoder then generates the caption one word at a time, each word conditioned on the words so far and on the image features, typically attending over those features through cross-attention so the model can ground each phrase in the relevant region of the picture. The decoder is trained to predict the next word of human-written reference captions, and at inference it generates autoregressively. Caption quality is scored against human references with overlap metrics, though these correlate only loosely with how good a human finds the description.

Modern image captioning has largely folded into the broader vision-language model. Rather than a bespoke captioner, the dominant approach connects a powerful pretrained image encoder, often one aligned with language by a model like CLIP, to a large language model, so the same system can caption an image, answer questions about it, or hold a conversation grounded in it, with captioning becoming just one instruction among many. This convergence reflects a general trend: specialized vision tasks are increasingly expressed as natural-language requests to a single multimodal model rather than as separate networks, with captioning the oldest and most direct example of image-to-text generation.

image.alt: An image captioning model: an input photo of a surfer is read by a convolutional encoder into a grid of image features, which a transformer decoder attends to through cross-attention while generating the caption one word at a time, starting from a start token and producing a surfer riding on.
image.caption: Image captioning joins a visual encoder that reads the picture to a language decoder that writes the sentence. The encoder turns the image into feature vectors, and the decoder attends to those features as it generates the caption word by word, the inverse of text-to-image generation.
